original_abstract: In this work, we developed an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) catalyst featuring atomically dispersed Mn sites for the direct production of hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), utilizing seawater as a sustainable resource. The catalyst demonstrated remarkable performance, generating up to 2230 μM H₂O₂ in 7 hours from alkaline water and 1800 μM from seawater under identical conditions. Notably, the material is highly stable and can be rapidly recovered and reused without a significant drop in activity. Our mechanistic investigations revealed that H₂O₂ is produced via a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) pathway—a departure from the standard oxygen reduction route. This process involves both direct and indirect WOR mechanisms: photoinduced holes (h⁺) either oxidize H₂O directly to H₂O₂ in a single 2e⁻ step or oxidize hydroxide ions (OH⁻) to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which subsequently combine to produce H₂O₂. The catalytic sites were characterized at the atomic level through a comprehensive suite of techniques, including EPR, XANES, EXAFS, HR-TEM, XPS, and MAS solid-state NMR. These experimental findings were further supported by multiscale molecular modeling, which integrated classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations with quantum chemical calculations to elucidate the unique behavior of the Mn active sites.
humanized_abstract: An atomically manganese (Mn) incorporated aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ) (AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 ) catalyst is prepared by hydrothermal method and used to generate hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) in situ from seawater. The prepared catalyst shows excellent catalytic performance, achieving up to 2230 μM H 2 O 2 in 7 h using alkaline water and 1800 μM H 2 O 2 in seawater under the same conditions. The as-prepared AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst shows good stability and can be quickly recovered and reused for several cycles without obvious decrease in the catalytic activity. In situ operando synchrotron absorption spectroscopy, X-ray scattering technique and solid-state NMR show that the AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst conducts a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) to generate hydrogen peroxide, involving both direct and indirect WOR pathways. Photoinduced holes (h + ) in the catalyst can either oxidize water (H 2 O) to hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) via a single two-electron process or act on hydroxide (OH - ) to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which react with each other to yield hydrogen peroxide. The atomic configuration of the active sites on AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst is characterized by a full set of structural analysis methods, including EPR, XANES, EXAFS, HR-TEM, XPS and MAS solid-state NMR. By comparing all the structural information, multiscale molecular modeling involving classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations and quantum calculations has been conducted to reveal the unique activity and selectivity of Mn active sites.

In situ operando synchrotron absorption spectroscopy, X-ray scattering technique and solid-state NMR show that the AN-Mn/C 3 N 4 catalyst conducts a two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) to generate hydrogen peroxide, involving both direct and indirect WOR pathways.
